title: Helpful to a Fault: Measuring Illicit Assistance in Multi-Turn, Multilingual LLM Agents
abstract: LLM-based agents execute real-world workflows via tools. These affordances enable ill-intended adversaries to also use these agents to carry out complex misuse scenarios. Existing agent-misuse benchmarks largely test single-prompt instructions, leaving a gap in measuring how agents end up helping with harmful or illegal tasks over multiple turns. We introduce **STING** (*Sequential Testing of Illicit N-step Goal execution*), an automated red-teaming framework that constructs a step-by-step illicit plan grounded in a benign persona and iteratively probes a target agent with adaptive follow-ups, using judge agents to track phase completion. We further introduce an analysis framework that models multi-turn red-teaming as a time-to-first-jailbreak random variable, enabling analysis tools like discovery curves, hazard-ratio attribution by attack language, and a new metric: Restricted Mean Jailbreak Discovery. Across AgentHarm scenarios, STING yields substantially higher illicit-task completion than single-turn prompting and chat-oriented multi-turn baselines adapted to tool-using agents. In multilingual evaluations across six non-English settings, we find that attack success and illicit-task completion do *not* consistently increase in lower-resource languages, diverging from common chatbot findings. Overall, STING provides a practical way to evaluate and stress-test agent misuse in realistic deployment settings, where interactions are inherently multi-turn and often multilingual. Our code is available at https://github.com/epfl-nlp/helpful-to-a-fault.
decision_comment:
This paper introduces STING, a multi-turn red-teaming framework for evaluating tool-using LLM agents, along with a survival-analysis-based evaluation methodology for measuring jailbreak dynamics.

The reviewers provided consistently positive scores (all Weak Accepts). They agreed that agent safety in multi-turn, multilingual settings is an important and timely problem, and appreciated both the framework design and the proposed analysis tools. The main concerns focused on judge reliability, potential confounds in the multilingual setup, and questions about novelty and evaluation breadth.

During rebuttal, the authors provided strong and targeted clarifications. They substantially expanded human validation of the judges, added native-language strategy experiments to address multilingual concerns, and included additional analyses on judge sensitivity and defense generalization. Reviewers acknowledged that these additions resolved their concerns and maintained or raised their scores.

While some limitations remain—such as reliance on a single benchmark and moderate novelty as a system integration—the paper offers a solid and practical contribution to agent safety evaluation. Given the strengthened empirical support and clear positive reviewer consensus, I recommend Accept.

reviews.summary: This paper introduces STING, an automated multi-turn red-teaming framework for tool-using LLM agents. A strategist decomposes harmful intents into phased attack plans, an attacker executes them conversationally against a target agent, and judge agents provide feedback. The paper also proposes a survival-analysis framework (time-to-first-jailbreak, Kaplan-Meier discovery curves, RMJD metric, Cox hazard ratios) and evaluates across 44 AgentHarm scenarios, 5 target models, and 7 languages. Key findings: multi-turn attacks substantially outperform single-turn prompting; non-English languages don't consistently increase jailbreak success (unlike chatbot settings); reasoning effort has non-monotonic effects on safety.
reviews.strengths_and_weaknesses:
Strengths:

The gap this paper targets is real and timely. Existing agent safety benchmarks like AgentHarm and OS-Harm only test single-turn malicious instructions, so we don't really know what happens when an adversary can adapt over multiple turns. The +107% AHS jump for Qwen3-Next in Table 1 makes the case clear — single-turn evaluations are missing a lot. The survival-analysis framework in Section 4 is probably the most interesting methodological piece. Importing Kaplan-Meier estimators and Cox proportional hazards into red-teaming evaluation is a smart move that gives you cost-aware comparisons rather than just raw success rates. I can see this being adopted by other papers regardless of whether they use STING itself.

Weakness:

The entire pipeline depends on LLM judges for refusal detection and phase completion, but the human validation is quite thin — 83 samples for the refusal judge and 56 for the intent checker. The refusal judge has 0.71 recall, which means it's missing roughly 30% of actual refusals. This could systematically inflate the attack success numbers. More importantly, this validation was only done on Urdu and Telugu conversations, but the judges are used across all seven languages. I'd want to see validation on a broader sample before fully trusting the multilingual results.

There's a confound in the multilingual setup that deserves more attention. Attack plans are generated in English by Gemini 3 Pro and then translated into the target language (Appendix A.4). So non-English attacks are really translated English strategies, not natively-conceived multilingual attacks. This could partly explain why non-English doesn't help — the attack quality may simply degrade through translation rather than this being a fundamental property of agentic settings. The paper should acknowledge this more directly.

The related work section overlooks relevant prior work on multi-turn agent safety. In particular, HaicoSystem (Zhou et al., 2024) already evaluates agent safety in multi-turn, tool-augmented settings with simulated social interactions. The claim in Section 2.2 that agentic safety benchmarks "typically issue a single-turn malicious instruction" should be qualified in light of this. The paper should discuss how STING's approach (adversarial phased decomposition with adaptive feedback) differs from HaicoSystem's simulation-based evaluation.
reviews.final_justification: Thanks, the response cleared my concerns.

reviews.summary: LLM agents are equipped with advanced tools and strong memory and can formulate workflows. However, these powerful tools might lead to illicit use and misuse scenarios. Existing benchmarks have addressed this problem by using single-prompt instructions to measure harmful behaviors. While this line of research has greatly advanced our understanding of agents, a gap still exists between single-prompt, single-language settings and multi-turn, multilingual settings, the latter being more practical in real-world interactions. To address this gap, the authors propose a sequential testing framework for illicit N-step goal execution, named STING, focusing on malicious and power-misuse scenarios of agents in multi-turn, multilingual settings. They designed four coordinated agents — a strategist, an attacker, and two judges — to test illicit task-completion probabilities. Results show that STING achieves up to 107.1% higher illicit task-completion rates than predominant single-prompt instructions. They also found that agents in lower-resource language scenarios do not necessarily exhibit increased jailbreak success probability, which differs from previous chatbot findings.
reviews.strengths_and_weaknesses:
Strengths:
•The multi-turn scenario breaks down the general jailbreak problem into several phases, which can significantly increase the difficulty for the target agent to recognize malicious behavior and thereby improve the attack success rate.

•The idea of comparison across multilingual settings is interesting. Different languages have different information densities, which might also influence the attack rate. For example, languages with lower information density might mislead the target agent, making it harder to understand and recognize malicious behavior.

•Extensive experiments are conducted, and ablation studies are performed on nearly all important variables and parameters, ensuring strict experimental control and thorough analysis.

Weaknesses:
•What is the basis for problem separation? I think the separation of phases is very important, so how to determine these separations and evaluate the quality of the separation should be clearly mentioned and addressed.

•Do different languages share the same phases? The information density varies across languages, and the length of prompts might also be a contributing factor to the results. In Table 2, the authors claim that language has a limited effect on jailbreak success, but I think the length of prompts for each phase may differ across languages. For lower-resource languages, more phases might be needed to achieve a successful jailbreak.

•The authors mention the difference between chatbot settings and tool-use settings. However, in the experiments, the baseline adopts the chatbot setting while the proposed method operates under the tool-use setting. This difference may require further explanation — specifically, which setting is more susceptible to jailbreaking and why?
reviews.key_questions_for_authors:
1: What is the basis for problem separation?
2: Do different languages share the same phases?
3: Which setting is more susceptible to jailbreaking and why?
reviews.limitations: No limitations and future works are included in the discussion.
reviews.final_justification: Thanks, the authors fully solved my concern.

reviews.summary:
This paper studies misuse evaluation for tool-using LLM agents in multi-turn settings. 
It introduces STING, where a strategist decomposes a harmful goal into phases, an attacker interacts with the target agent over multiple turns, and two judge modules detect refusals and phase completion. 
The paper evaluates STING on AgentHarm across multiple models and languages, compares against single-turn prompting and X-Teaming, and proposes an analysis framework based on time-to-first-jailbreak, including discovery curves, hazard ratios, and RMJD. 
The main empirical claim is that multi-turn red-teaming substantially increases harmful-task completion relative to single-turn prompting, while multilingual effects are weaker than prior chatbot-focused work suggests.
reviews.strengths_and_weaknesses:
1. The paper addresses an important problem and the proposed multi-turn setup is more realistic than standard single-turn agent misuse evaluation. The experimental study is reasonably broad, and the paper includes useful ablations on reasoning effort, tool hints, and lightweight defenses.

2. My main concern is that the central empirical conclusions are not as cleanly supported as the paper suggests. The multilingual claims are confounded because attack plans are generated in English and then reused for all languages, which makes it hard to know whether the results reflect genuine multilingual safety behavior or artifacts of an English-centric attack pipeline. In addition, the evaluation relies heavily on automated judges, yet the reported human validation is limited and the refusal judge has only moderate recall, which could materially affect the reported jailbreak dynamics.

3. The novelty and practical significance feel more incremental than the paper claims. STING combines known ingredients—decomposition, adaptive multi-turn attacks, judge-based evaluation, and AgentHarm-style grading—into an agent setting, but the conceptual advance over recent multi-turn jailbreak and agent-safety work is modest. The new analysis layer is interesting, but it is not yet clear that RMJD or the hazard-based analysis changes conclusions in a meaningful way beyond more standard attack-success summaries. Combined with limited defense evaluation and some fairness issues in the setup, this leaves me unconvinced that the contribution is strong enough for acceptance in its current form.
reviews.key_questions_for_authors:
1. The multilingual conclusions seem potentially confounded by generating plans in English and reusing them across all attack languages. Can the authors show results when the strategist also plans directly in each target language? A convincing control here would substantially strengthen the multilingual claim.

2. How sensitive are the reported ASR / RMJD conclusions to judge errors, especially given the refusal judge’s reported recall of 0.71 on a relatively small validation set? I would like to see a larger human audit and an analysis of whether model rankings or language conclusions change after adjudication.

3. The comparison to X-Teaming is somewhat hard to interpret because the paper explicitly notes that ASR is not directly comparable, and X-Teaming was designed for chat rather than tool-using agents. Can the authors provide a stronger agent-specific baseline or a more controlled adaptation?

4. The defense study is limited to Qwen3-Next. Do the main conclusions about prompt filtering versus safety prompting generalize to other target models? This would affect the practical significance of the defense findings.
reviews.final_justification: My concerns have been adequately addressed.

reviews.summary: This paper studies safety risks in LLM-based agents that interact with users and tools over multiple turns. The authors propose STING, an automated red-teaming framework that simulates adversarial users by decomposing harmful objectives into sequential phases executed through multi-turn interactions. The paper also introduces an evaluation approach that models jailbreak discovery as a time-to-event process and proposes the Restricted Mean Jailbreak Discovery (RMJD) metric. Experiments on the AgentHarm benchmark show that multi-turn attacks significantly increase illicit task completion compared with single-turn prompting and reveal differences in multilingual safety behavior.
reviews.strengths_and_weaknesses:
This paper addresses an important and timely problem: evaluating safety vulnerabilities in agentic LLM systems that interact with tools and users over multiple turns. As LLM agents become increasingly capable and autonomous, understanding how harmful tasks can emerge through incremental multi-turn interactions is a meaningful research direction. The proposed STING framework provides a structured approach for automated red-teaming by decomposing harmful objectives into phases and coordinating multiple agents that simulate adversarial users. The experimental design is reasonably comprehensive, evaluating several models, multilingual settings, and mitigation strategies. Additionally, the paper introduces an interesting analysis perspective by framing jailbreak discovery as a survival analysis problem, allowing the authors to derive discovery curves and the RMJD metric to quantify how quickly vulnerabilities are exposed. This methodological framing may be useful for future work studying safety evaluation under constrained testing budgets. 

However, the paper also has several limitations. First, although the STING framework is clearly described, much of its architecture resembles existing automated red-teaming or jailbreak-generation approaches. The novelty lies mainly in system integration and evaluation methodology rather than fundamentally new algorithms. Second, the experimental evaluation relies heavily on the AgentHarm dataset, which may limit the generality of the conclusions. It remains unclear whether the observed failure modes would persist across other agent benchmarks or real-world deployment environments. Third, the proposed RMJD metric summarizes discovery curves but may not add substantial insight beyond the underlying curves themselves; the paper could further justify why this metric is necessary or how it provides unique interpretability advantages. Finally, while the authors evaluate a simple defense mechanism, the analysis of defenses remains relatively shallow, and stronger mitigation strategies or comparisons with existing safety frameworks would strengthen the practical implications of the work.
reviews.key_questions_for_authors:
The experiments rely primarily on the AgentHarm benchmark. Have the authors tested STING on other agent evaluation datasets or real-world task environments to verify that the observed vulnerabilities generalize?

STING uses multiple LLM components (strategist, attacker, evaluators). How sensitive are the results to the specific models used for these roles? Would weaker attacker models still produce similar results?

The paper evaluates a simple safety prompt as a mitigation strategy. Have the authors tested other defenses, such as policy filters, tool-access restrictions, or structured reasoning safeguards?
reviews.limitations: Yes. The authors explicitly discuss potential dual-use risks of the framework, acknowledging that techniques for decomposing multi-turn attacks could potentially be misused to improve adversarial strategies. They also note that their work focuses on evaluation rather than providing operational attack instructions and recommend responsible release practices.
